explanation: Sexual reproduction in plants. Flowering plants are the most widespread types of plants in different environments around the world. They are the most important types of plants to human. G.R. Because human get his food from them. Flowering plants ensure the continuity of their species through sexual reproduction, which is carried out by flowers. The image shows a large, vibrant orange and yellow flower representing a typical flowering plant.

The flower: It is the organ of sexual reproduction in flowering plants and it is a short stem whose leaves are modified to perform the reproductive function. Origin of the flowers: The flower arises from a vegetative bud which emerges from the axil of a leaf called bract. Bract: The leaf which the vegetative bud emerges from its axil and develops into a flower. The bud undergoes modifications to develop into a flower which performs the reproductive function.

The image shows a diagram of a plant stem where the flower originates from a vegetative bud. It labels the terminal bud at the top, an axillary bud on the side, and the bract, which is a small leaf-like structure at the base of the flower stalk. Inflorescences: A part of the plant stem may carry a cluster (group) of flowers, called an inflorescence and the part of the stem that carries it is called the peduncle.

Inflorescence: A cluster of flowers carried by the peduncle. Peduncle: The part of the stem that carries a cluster of flowers. Take care: The components of the flower in the inflorescence are represented as follows: Flower, Pedicel, Bract, and Peduncle. A small diagram illustrates these parts, showing the peduncle as the main stalk and the pedicel as the individual flower stalk.

There are multiple types of inflorescences, three of them are presented in the following table comparing Simple raceme, Simple corymb, and Simple umbel inflorescences. The table includes illustrative figures for each type showing the arrangement of flowers on the peduncle.

Simple raceme inflorescences: One long main peduncle continues to grow. Flowers emerge from all directions and each has a small stalk (pedicel). Direction of opening (blooming) of flowers is from the bottom (the older flowers) to the top (the younger flowers). General shape resembles a cluster of grapes. Examples include the Stock plant and Snapdragon plant. An image shows purple Snapdragon flowers arranged along a tall stalk.

Simple corymb inflorescences: One short main peduncle stops growing after forming a number of flowers. Flowers emerge on both sides of the peduncle and all are almost at the same level. Direction of opening is from the outside (the older flowers) to the inside (the younger flowers). General shape resembles a comb. Examples include the Cherry plant and Iberis (candytuft) plant. An image shows white Iberis flowers forming a flat-topped cluster.

Simple umbel inflorescences: All pedicels emerge from one point at the end of the peduncle. Flowers emerge from one point and their pedicels are of equal length. Direction of opening is from the outside (the older flowers) to the inside (the younger flowers). General shape resembles a tent or an umbrella. Examples include the Garlic plant and Onion plant. An image shows a spherical cluster of white Onion flowers.

Question Answer: Q: What is the reason for naming the following inflorescences with these names? (1) Simple raceme inflorescence. (2) Simple corymb inflorescence. (3) Simple umbel inflorescence. A: (1) The emergence of flowers from all directions in the simple raceme inflorescence, each with a small pedicel, gives it a shape similar to a cluster of grapes. (2) The emergence of flowers from both sides of the peduncle in the simple corymb inflorescence and they are almost at the same level, gives it a shape similar to a comb. (3) The emergence of flowers from a single point at the end of the peduncle of the simple umbel inflorescence, with their pedicel being equal in length, gives it a shape similar to a tent or an umbrella.

Structure of the flower: The flower consists of a slender stalk called the pedicel ending in a swollen part called the receptacle, which carries the floral leaves arranged in groups, each called a floral whorl. Receptacle: A swollen part at the end of the flower pedicel that carries the floral whorls. The arrangement of floral whorls from outside to inside in a typical flower is: 1. Calyx, 2. Corolla, 3. Stamens (Androecium), 4. Pistil (Gynoecium).

The four floral whorls can be identified by examining a longitudinal section in a flower. The provided diagram shows: Stamen (consisting of Anther and Filament), Pistil (consisting of Stigma, Style, and Ovary containing an Ovule), Corolla (Petal), Calyx (Sepal), Receptacle, and Pedicel. The diagram illustrates how these parts are layered from the outer sepal to the central pistil.

Calyx: The first (outer) floral whorl. Structure: Green leaves called sepals. Their number varies from one flower to another. Function: Protection of the inner parts of the flower, especially before blooming. The image shows a flower bud with green sepals enclosing the developing petals.

Corolla: The second floral whorl that lies internal to the calyx. Structure: Delicate white or coloured leaves called petals that are often fragrant to attract insects. Their number is usually equal to the number of sepals, and alternates with them in position. Function: 1. Protection of the reproductive organs of the flower. 2. Attracting insects that play an important role in completing the process of floral reproduction. The image shows bright orange petals emerging from the calyx.

Take care: (1) The sepals may be separate (not attached to each other) or fused. (2) The petals may be separate arranged in several whorls or fused and arranged in a single whorl, as shown in the following table. Egyptian rose: Separate sepals (not attached to each other) and separate petals (arranged in several whorls). Petunia: Fused sepals and fused petals (arranged in one whorl). The page includes photos of a red Egyptian rose and a pink Petunia flower.

(3) In some flowers, sepals cannot be distinguished from petals based on their colour or appearance; calyx and corolla in these flowers are collectively referred to as the perianth, such as in the daffodil flower. An image shows a yellow daffodil flower. (4) Some flowers lack both sepals and petals, and are known as naked flowers, such as the Willow flower. An image shows the fuzzy catkins of a Willow flower.

Stamens (Androecium): The third floral whorl that lies just inside the corolla. Structure: The male organ of the flower consists of several modified leaves called stamens. Each stamen consists of a thin filament that ends with a swelling called the anther. The anther usually contains two lobes, each with two sacs filled with fine grains called pollen grains. Function: Production of pollen grains. G.R. The stamen is the male organ in the flower because it produces pollen grains (male gametes). A diagram shows a stamen with its filament and anther releasing pollen grains.

Pistil (Gynoecium): The fourth floral whorl (located at the center of the flower). Structure: The female organ of the flower consists of one or more leaves called carpels, each carpel resembles a flask. Each carpel consists of a swollen base called the ovary, connected to a slender tube called the style, ending with a sticky surface known as the stigma. Function: Production of ovules. G.R. The pistil is the female organ in the flower because it produces ovules (female gametes). A diagram shows a carpel with the stigma, style, and ovary containing an ovule.

The benefits of flowers for human include medical benefits. As flowers contain antioxidants and volatile oils, as in: 1. Chamomile flowers: Used to calm the nervous system. 2. Hibiscus flowers: Used to lower high blood pressure. 3. Lavender flowers: Used to relieve muscle pain and headache. Images show a cup of chamomile tea, a glass of hibiscus drink, and a glass of lavender drink with sprigs of the respective flowers.

Industrial benefits: Petals of some flowers, such as rose flowers, are used in the production of rose water and rose oil in some villages of Fayoum Governorate. An image shows bottles of rose oil next to pink rose blossoms.

Medical awareness; Hay fever: It is an inflammation caused by an immune response to harmless substances, such as pollen grains. Its symptoms: Frequent sneezing and watery eyes. Prevention methods: This condition can be reduced by avoiding areas with high levels (concentrations) of pollen grains. An image shows a woman sneezing while holding a dandelion seed head.

Typical flower: The flower is called typical if it contains the 4 floral whorls. Typical flower: The flower whose floral leaves are arranged in 4 floral whorls. G.R. Egyptian rose flower is a typical flower because it contains the 4 floral whorls. A diagram of a typical flower labels the stigma, style, ovary, carpel, petal, anther, filament, stamen, sepal, ovules, receptacle, and pedicel.

The sex of flower: The sex of the flower varies depending on whether it contains male organs, female organs, or both together. So it may be: Bisexual flower or Unisexual flower. Bisexual flower: The flower that contains both male and female organs. An image shows a red bisexual flower with both stamens and a central pistil.
Unisexual flower: The flower that has either male organs only or female organs only. Images show a female flower (containing only a green pistil) and a male flower (containing only yellow stamens).

Compare between the bisexual flower, the male flower, and the female flower. Bisexual flower: Reproductive organs are stamens and pistil; Symbol is ⚥; Shape shows both organs; Number of floral whorls is 4; Examples: Flowers of tomato and eggplant. Male flower: Reproductive organs are stamens only; Symbol is ♂; Shape shows only stamens; Number of floral whorls is 3; Examples: Flowers of palm, maize (corn) and pumpkin. Female flower: Reproductive organs are pistil only; Symbol is ♀; Shape shows only the pistil; Number of floral whorls is 3; Examples: Flowers of palm, maize (corn) and pumpkin.

G.R. (1) The typical flower is a bisexual flower because it contains both the male organ (stamens) and the female organ (pistil). (2) The flower of the palm plant is a unisexual flower because it contains only the male organ (stamens) or the female organ (pistil).
